story.message: Scene scene-4a-popular-table has no choice attached, but its defaultNextScene points to scene-4a-defend-aftermath, which is not a valid next scene. The scene should either have a choice with options leading to valid scenes, or defaultNextScene should point to a scene that exists in the scenes array. — Suggested fix: Either: (a) Create a choice with id 'choice-3a' that branches to scene-4a-defend-aftermath and scene-4a-join-aftermath based on player action, OR (b) Remove the defaultNextScene and attach the choice via the choices array. The current structure is ambiguous. (location: scenes[3].defaultNextScene)

Choice choice-3a references scene-4a-popular-table as its scene, but the choice's options point to scene-4a-defend-aftermath and scene-4a-join-aftermath. However, scene-4a-popular-table itself has a defaultNextScene pointing to scene-4a-defend-aftermath. This creates ambiguity: does the player make a choice at scene-4a-popular-table, or does the scene auto-transition? — Suggested fix: Remove defaultNextScene from scene-4a-popular-table (line in scenes[3]), since choice-3a is the intended branching mechanism. The choice will handle all transitions. (location: choices[2] and scenes[3])

Choice choice-3a has 3 options, but only 2 unique nextScene values are specified. Option [2] (id 'c') points to 'scene-5-project-assignment', which skips the aftermath scenes entirely. This is inconsistent with options [0] and [1], which lead to aftermath scenes. — Suggested fix: Either: (a) Create a third aftermath scene (e.g., scene-4a-deflect-aftermath) for the 'change subject' option, OR (b) Redirect option [2] to one of the existing aftermath scenes. Currently, this path skips narrative resolution. (location: choices[2].options[2])

Scene scene-4b-sam-table has defaultNextScene 'scene-5-project-assignment', but it also has a choice attached (choice-3b). Per the data model, a scene should use EITHER a choice OR a defaultNextScene, not both. This creates ambiguity about which transition mechanism fires. — Suggested fix: Remove the defaultNextScene field from scene-4b-sam-table. Choice-3b will handle all transitions from this scene. (location: scenes[4])

Choice choice-3b is attached to scene-4b-sam-table, but all 3 options point to the same nextScene: 'scene-5-project-assignment'. This means the choice has no functional impact on the story flow — all paths converge immediately. — Suggested fix: Either: (a) Create branching aftermath scenes for each option (e.g., scene-4b-affirm-aftermath, scene-4b-quiet-aftermath, scene-4b-invite-aftermath), OR (b) Remove choice-3b and rely on defaultNextScene. Currently, the choice is cosmetic. (location: choices[3].options[*].nextScene)

Scene scene-4c-new-table has defaultNextScene 'scene-5-project-assignment', but it also has a choice attached (choice-3c). Per the data model, a scene should use EITHER a choice OR a defaultNextScene, not both. — Suggested fix: Remove the defaultNextScene field from scene-4c-new-table. Choice-3c will handle all transitions. (location: scenes[5])

Choice choice-3c has 3 options, but option [2] (id 'c', 'Admit you're curious about Jordan's table too') points to nextScene 'scene-4a-popular-table'. This creates a loop: the player can choose to sit at the empty table (scene-4c), then immediately choose to go to Jordan's table (scene-4a), which they could have chosen in choice-2. This is narratively confusing and breaks the 'one choice per scene' flow. — Suggested fix: Change option [2] to point to scene-5-project-assignment (or create a new transition scene). If the player wants to join Jordan's table, that choice should happen at the lunchroom (choice-2), not after committing to the empty table. (location: choices[4].options[2].nextScene)

The traitMapping in choice-3a, option [2] (change subject) has ADAPTABILITY: 2, but this option is described as 'Change the subject smoothly and move the conversation on.' This is a valid adaptability move, but the nextScene points to scene-5-project-assignment, which skips the aftermath entirely. This means the player's choice to 'change the subject' has no narrative consequence — the scene just ends. This is inconsistent with options [0] and [1], which lead to aftermath scenes that show the consequence of the choice. — Suggested fix: Create scene-4a-deflect-aftermath to show the consequence of changing the subject (e.g., Sam goes quiet, the moment passes, but the tension lingers). Then update option [2] to point to this new scene. (location: choices[2].options[2])

Choice choice-3b (attached to scene-4b-sam-table) has 3 options with different text descriptions, but all 3 options point to the same nextScene ('scene-5-project-assignment'). This means the choice is purely cosmetic — it affects trait tracking but not story flow. Additionally, the choice's traitMapping shows different trait changes for each option (e.g., option [0] has EMPATHY: 2, CONFIDENCE: 1; option [1] has ADAPTABILITY: 2, EMPATHY: 1; option [2] has CONFIDENCE: 2, EMPATHY: 1), which suggests the choices should have narrative consequences. But they don't. — Suggested fix: Create 3 branching aftermath scenes (scene-4b-affirm-aftermath, scene-4b-quiet-aftermath, scene-4b-invite-aftermath) and update the choice options to point to these scenes. This will make the choice meaningful in both trait tracking and story flow. (location: choices[3])

Scene 4 (lunchroom choice) is a critical bottleneck with three heavily branching paths (4a, 4b, 4c) that collapse into scene 5 with no meaningful differentiation in setup. Players experience wildly different emotional contexts arriving at the same project assignment. — Suggested fix: Create 3 distinct scene-5 variants that open with brief contextual callbacks: (5a) 'Ms. Alvarez calls your name and you realize you're in a group with the person you just defended/ignored,' (5b) 'Sam's already at the table when you arrive,' (5c) 'Alex is somehow also in this group.' Even 2-3 lines of context-specific dialogue would anchor players' emotional continuity. (location: Scenes 3-5 transition (lunchroom → project assignment))

Choice 3a (Sam's tray incident) presents a false dilemma. Options (a) defend Sam, (b) laugh along, and (c) change subject all lead to the same scene-5, but the emotional weight and character relationship consequences are vastly different. The choice feels consequential but the game doesn't track or reflect those consequences until much later (if at all). — Suggested fix: Add immediate, visible consequences in the same scene: (a) Sam makes eye contact and nods; Jordan's smile tightens slightly. (b) Sam doesn't look back; Jordan's arm around Maya feels heavier. (c) Awkward beat where nobody quite knows what just happened. Make these micro-reactions visible so players feel the weight of their choice immediately. (location: Scene 4a, Choice 3a)

The project assignment (scene 5) is completely non-interactive. Ms. Alvarez reads groups off a clipboard with no player input, and the 'random' grouping is actually predetermined. While this is narratively clever (Alvarez is deliberately engineering outcomes), it removes player agency at a critical moment and doesn't give players the information they need to understand why this matters. — Suggested fix: Option 1 (agency-focused): Let players choose their group from available options before Alvarez reads the list. Option 2 (narrative-focused): Have Alvarez explain her reasoning in a way that makes the grouping feel earned, not arbitrary—'I'm putting you three together because I think you need each other.' Option 3 (hybrid): Keep the non-choice but add a reaction beat where Maya realizes why this group was chosen, with internal dialogue acknowledging the setup. (location: Scene 5, Ms. Alvarez's dialogue)

The three branch paths (authentic connection, fit-in, bridge-builder) are defined by early lunch choices but the game doesn't clearly signal which path players are on. Players replaying won't have clear guidance on how to reach different endings, making intentional path selection difficult. — Suggested fix: Add subtle UI/narrative signposting: After major choice moments, include brief internal monologue that signals which path Maya is on ('I'm building something new' vs. 'I'm fitting in' vs. 'I'm being real with one person'). This helps players understand the consequences of their choices and makes intentional replays possible. (location: Scenes 3-8 (all branch paths))

Sam Okafor's characterization relies heavily on quiet, withdrawn, self-protective traits without sufficient complexity or agency. Risk of portraying Black male character as inherently passive/victimized rather than multidimensional. — Recommendation: Expand Sam's agency: Give him moments where he initiates connection, makes bold choices, or leads rather than follows. Add scenes showing his humor/wit in contexts where he's not defending himself. Show his drawing/creative work as a source of power, not just coping. Consider a scene where Sam makes a choice that affects the group, not just receives kindness. (location: Character profile: Sam Okafor; scenes 2, 4b, 6)

The 'defending Sam' choice (scene-4a-defend-aftermath) is framed as morally correct, but the narrative doesn't adequately address the systemic nature of Jordan's behavior or hold her accountable. Kindness from Maya is presented as sufficient resolution. — Recommendation: Add a follow-up scene where Maya or another character addresses Jordan's behavior directly—not as punishment, but as accountability. Show Jordan having to reckon with the impact of her actions, not just move on. Consider a later scene where Jordan either changes behavior or faces social consequence. Make clear that 'being nice to the victim' ≠ 'solving the problem.' (location: Scenes 4a, 4a-defend-aftermath, 4a-join-aftermath)

Alex Torres-Mireles is the only character with explicitly non-binary pronouns (they/them), but their identity is never directly acknowledged or explored in the narrative. Risk of tokenization—diversity present but not integrated. — Recommendation: Include at least one natural moment where Alex's pronouns are used in dialogue with acknowledgment (not explanation—just normalization). Example: 'Alex and I are working on...' or a character naturally using they/them in conversation. Could be as simple as Maya internally noting 'Alex uses they/them' when first meeting, then using it naturally. This normalizes pronoun usage for all players. (location: Character profile: Alex; all scenes featuring Alex)

Devon Park is the only character with an explicitly Asian surname (Park), but his ethnicity/cultural background is never mentioned, explored, or integrated into his character. He exists as 'the competitive one' without cultural dimension. — Recommendation: Either: (1) Integrate cultural elements naturally (family dinner conversation about expectations, cultural context for his competitiveness, etc.), OR (2) Deliberately diversify his characterization to show he's competitive for reasons unrelated to cultural pressure. Don't require him to 'represent' his culture, but do acknowledge it exists. Could be a single line about his family or background that shows dimension beyond achievement. (location: Character profile: Devon Park; all scenes)

The 'stay quiet' choice (scene-6, choice-4:c) is presented as a negative outcome, but the narrative doesn't adequately explain WHY speaking up matters beyond 'Sam deserves it.' Misses opportunity to model assertiveness as a value, not just kindness. — Recommendation: Add internal monologue or follow-up dialogue that explicitly frames speaking up as: (1) advocating for good ideas, not just defending people, and (2) a skill worth developing. Show that silence has costs—not just for Sam, but for the group's work and for Maya's sense of integrity. Make the ethical principle clear: 'Good ideas deserve to be heard, regardless of who suggests them.' (location: Scene 6, choice-4 options and responses)

dialogue.message: Choice choice-4 is attached to scene-6-project-tension, but the scene's description and dialogue suggest the player is making a choice about whether to defend Sam's idea. However, the choice's traitMapping shows option [2] (staying quiet) has negative trait changes (CONFIDENCE: -2, EMPATHY: -1), which is appropriate. But the nextScene for all 3 options is 'scene-7-reflection', meaning the choice has no branching impact on the story — only on trait tracking. — Suggested fix: Consider creating branching aftermath scenes (e.g., scene-6-defended-aftermath, scene-6-compromised-aftermath, scene-6-silent-aftermath) to show narrative consequences of the choice. Alternatively, if all paths converge, remove the choice and rely on defaultNextScene for clarity. (location: choices[5].options[*].nextScene)

Choice choice-5 is attached to scene-7-reflection, and its 3 options point to 3 different nextScene values (scene-8a-end-friendship, scene-8b-end-crowd, scene-8c-end-bridge). However, all 3 of these scenes have defaultNextScene: 'END'. This is correct. BUT: the branchDialogue array references branch IDs (branch-authentic-connection, branch-fit-in-first, branch-bridge-builder, branch-defended-then-drifted) that should be triggered by the outcomes array. The outcomes array DOES contain these branch IDs, and their triggeredBy lists reference choice paths. This is correct, but the mapping is complex and should be verified. — Suggested fix: No fix needed for this specific issue, but note that the branch-triggered-by logic is complex. Verify that the choice paths in outcomes[*].triggeredBy correctly map to the player's actual decision history. (location: outcomes[*].triggeredBy and branchDialogue[*].branchId)

Outcome branch-defended-then-drifted is defined in the outcomes array with triggeredBy: ['choice-2:a', 'choice-3a:a', 'choice-5:b'], but there is no corresponding branchDialogue entry for this branch. The other 3 branches (branch-authentic-connection, branch-fit-in-first, branch-bridge-builder) all have branchDialogue entries. — Suggested fix: Add a branchDialogue entry for branch-defended-then-drifted with appropriate dialogue lines for the ending scene (likely scene-8b-end-crowd, since the branch ends with choice-5:b). (location: outcomes[3] vs branchDialogue[*])

Scene scene-4a-popular-table is described as a scene where 'the player makes a choice' (Sam's tray is on the floor, what do you do?), but the scene itself does NOT have a 'choices' field embedded. Per the data model, the choice is referenced via the choices array, not embedded in the scene. However, the scene's dialogue includes a NARRATOR line that says 'The whole table looks at Maya. Waiting to see which way she tips.' This implies a choice is coming, but the scene's transition mechanism is unclear (defaultNextScene vs. choice-3a). — Suggested fix: Remove defaultNextScene from scene-4a-popular-table. The choice-3a mechanism will handle all transitions. (location: scenes[3])

Choice choice-3c (attached to scene-4c-new-table) has 3 options, but option [2] points back to scene-4a-popular-table, creating a narrative loop. The player chooses to sit at the empty table (scene-4c), then immediately chooses to go to Jordan's table (scene-4a). This breaks the linear flow and suggests the player made the wrong choice at the lunchroom (choice-2). This is confusing. — Suggested fix: Change option [2] to point to scene-5-project-assignment. If the player wants to join Jordan's table, that choice should happen at the lunchroom (choice-2), not after committing to the empty table. (location: choices[4].options[2])

Choice choice-4 (attached to scene-6-project-tension) has 3 options with different text and traitMapping, but all 3 options point to the same nextScene ('scene-7-reflection'). This means the choice is purely cosmetic — it affects trait tracking but not story flow. The scene's dialogue suggests a high-stakes moment ('Maya has the floor. Exactly one sentence's worth, before Devon's plan just... becomes the plan.'), but the choice has no narrative consequence. — Suggested fix: Create 3 branching aftermath scenes (scene-6-defended-aftermath, scene-6-compromised-aftermath, scene-6-silent-aftermath) and update the choice options to point to these scenes. This will make the choice meaningful in both trait tracking and story flow. (location: choices[5])

The outcomes array defines 4 branches, but the branchDialogue array only has 3 entries. Specifically, branch-defended-then-drifted (outcomes[3]) has no corresponding branchDialogue entry. This means if the player triggers this branch (via choice-2:a, choice-3a:a, choice-5:b), the ending scene (scene-8b-end-crowd) will not have branch-specific dialogue variants. — Suggested fix: Add a branchDialogue entry for branch-defended-then-drifted. This branch represents a player who defended Sam but then drifted back to Jordan's table, so the dialogue should reflect that internal conflict. (location: outcomes[3] vs branchDialogue[*])
